narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—dreaming of law school and then facing this prediction of a 43% chance of not passing the bar exam feels like a gut punch, especially when you've poured so much into your path. Our model looked at your profile holistically, like piecing together a story of what often leads students to thrive or stumble on that final hurdle, and it saw patterns that tipped toward caution. The biggest signal was your Year 3 decile of 3.0, which stood out as the strongest pull toward struggle, suggesting that even if you started stronger in Year 1 at decile 4.0, your performance trended downward in a way that's historically tough to rebound from for bar success. Layered on that, your LSAT score of 30.0 raised similar flags, as it often correlates with the kind of foundational gaps that make the bar's intensity overwhelming. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.2 added to that narrative, painting a picture of solid but not standout academic momentum coming into law school. Even with your upper-middle family income, which might offer some support, it didn't outweigh those core academic signals—in fact, it nudged slightly toward the riskier side in the patterns we've seen. Your background as a white female pursuing full-time studies fit into broader trends that, combined with everything else, didn't shift the story toward passing odds. It's not that any one piece doomed you, but together, they formed a profile where too many students in similar spots have faced bar failure, prompting us to recommend against admission to protect your time and dreams. This isn't the end of your story—you could target schools that align better with bolstering those key areas, like LSAT prep or grade recovery strategies. You've got resilience in aiming for law in the first place; use this as fuel to rewrite the next chapter. We're rooting for you.
